notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your $2,350/month rent at Cascade Apartments is right at the heart of Seattle's 2026 rental market, and there's actually quite a bit worth checking before you sign that renewal. Let me walk through the most relevant angles, from largest potential savings to smallest.
Income-eligibility programs
The biggest lever—if you qualify—is Seattle's Multifamily Tax Exemption (MFTE) Program 7. This caps rent on set-aside units in newer buildings. The key question is where your income lands relative to the 2026 Area Median Income (AMI) for King County.
Based on your transactions, I can see your Microsoft base pay comes in at $2,500 semi-monthly (gross), which works out to roughly $60,000/year in base salary. You also had a $1,200 freelance deposit in May and $42,000 in RSU vesting—so your total 2026 income picture is meaningfully higher than base pay alone.
Here's how to check eligibility:
60% AMI threshold (1-person household): $69,060/year. If your total income stays below this, MFTE units cap your 1-bedroom rent at $1,850/month—saving you $500/month ($6,000/year) versus your current $2,350. Realistically, with RSUs and freelance income, you likely exceed this threshold in a high-income year.
80% AMI threshold (1-person household): $92,080/year. At 80% AMI, the MFTE cap on a 1-bedroom is $2,467/month—which is actually above your current rent, so this tier wouldn't reduce your cost.
Takeaway on MFTE: If your RSU vesting pushes your 2026 income above $69,060 (which it almost certainly does), MFTE units won't help this cycle. However, it's worth applying to the Seattle Housing Authority voucher lottery for future years, especially if your income varies.
Lease renewal negotiation tactics
This is where you have the most actionable leverage right now, given Seattle's specific 2026 legal environment.
The 180-day notice rule is your friend. Seattle law requires landlords to give 180 days' written notice before any rent increase. If Cascade Apartments hasn't formally served you that notice yet, they cannot legally raise your rent until 180 days after they do. This gives you time and leverage.
The 9.683% state cap (HB 1217) means the maximum Cascade could legally charge is approximately $2,577/month on renewal—so if they propose anything higher, that's a compliance issue you can flag immediately.
Negotiation approach to try:
Pull current listings on Zillow and Apartments.com for comparable 1-bedrooms in your Seattle neighborhood. The 2026 market average for a 1-bedroom is roughly $2,100–$2,250, which is below your current rent. That's meaningful leverage.
Check Cascade's own website for what they're offering new tenants on the same floor plan. Seattle landlords face restrictions on certain forms of price discrimination, and if new tenants are paying less than you, that's your strongest card.
Come to the conversation asking for a rent reduction to $2,100–$2,150, citing comparable market rates. If they push back, offer to meet in the middle around $2,200. The landlord's vacancy cost (roughly one month's rent, or ~$2,350) gives them strong incentive to retain a reliable tenant.
Concession alternative: If they won't budge on base rent, ask for one month free on the renewal term, or waiver of any parking or amenity fees. One month free on a 12-month lease is effectively $196/month in savings when amortized.
Estimated savings from negotiation: Bringing rent from $2,350 down to $2,150 saves $200/month ($2,400/year). A one-month concession saves roughly $196/month on an annualized basis.
Move-in cost and payment protections
If you're considering moving to a new unit rather than renewing, Seattle law caps your upfront costs significantly:
Combined security deposit and fees cannot exceed one month's rent
Move-in fees are capped at 10% of first month's rent (~$235 at your current rate)
You have the right to pay deposits and move-in fees in monthly installments—no income verification required to use this right
Emergency rental assistance (if needed)
United Way of King County offers up to $10,000 for households at or below 80% AMI who owe back rent. Given your income level, this likely doesn't apply in a standard year—but if your freelance or RSU income is irregular and you face a gap, it's worth knowing.
Your data checklist before renewal
Confirm your estimated 2026 total gross income (base + freelance + RSU fair market value at vest) to see if MFTE eligibility is realistic
Pull 3–5 comparable active 1-bedroom listings in your Seattle neighborhood this week
Check Cascade's website for current "new tenant" pricing on your floor plan
Verify whether you've received a formal 180-day notice of any increase—if not, you're in a stronger position than you may realize
Prepare a brief "tenant resume" noting consistent on-time rent payments
The single most impactful step you can take before renewing is checking comparable listings and using that data in a direct conversation with your property manager. A $200/month reduction is entirely realistic in the current Seattle market given the supply environment and the tenant protections now in place.
Would you like help estimating what a lower rent would do for your monthly cash flow and savings rate given your current income and expense picture?

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.

$10K
